mnemonics.title: Array Object Access
mnemonics.content: DORA - Dot Operator for Object, use Right-side Index with Arrow for pointer access. Remember: 'Dot for value, Arrow for address'

mnemonics.title: Constructor Call Rule
mnemonics.content: CARS - Constructor Always Runs for each element in array. Each object needs its own constructor invocation.

mnemonics.title: Memory Allocation
mnemonics.content: SARA - Stack Array: Right-side Auto-cleanup. Heap Array: Remember to delete[] always.

mnemonics.title: Object Initialization
mnemonics.content: PADI - Parameterized Array needs Default or Individual initialization. If parameterized constructor, either provide all values or add default constructor.

acronyms.usage: When you need multiple instances of a class stored contiguously: Student s[50]; stores 50 student records.
When you need polymorphism or dynamic object creation: Student* ptr[5]; allows each pointer to point to different derived class objects.
With arrays of objects, constructors acquire resources and destructors release them automatically when array goes out of scope.

visualTips.visualization: Draw boxes side-by-side: [Object1][Object2][Object3]... Each box contains the data members of that class. Like houses in a row - each house (object) has its own rooms (members).
Think of constructor as 'building' each object. For s[5], it's like building 5 houses one by one. Each house gets built using the constructor blueprint.
Array index [0] → First object, [1] → Second object. Like floor numbers in a building: Floor 0, Floor 1, Floor 2... Each floor (index) has an apartment (object).
Think of array name as address card. Passing arr means giving the address card. Using &arr makes it clear you're sharing the actual building, not copying it.

analogies.analogy: Think of a parking lot. Each parking space (array element) can hold a car (object). Just as cars have properties (color, model), objects have data members. The parking lot management is like the class definition.
When you buy 5 identical houses in a housing society, each house is built using the same blueprint (constructor), but each is a separate living space (object instance).
Like rooms in a hotel corridor - all rooms (objects) are next to each other, making it easy to find any room (object) by knowing its room number (index).

Array of objects is like owning 5 cars parked in your driveway. Array of pointers is like having 5 parking tickets (addresses) to cars that could be anywhere - in different parking lots or even different cities.
